skill.name: outlook-calendar-automation
skill.description: Automate Outlook Calendar tasks through Rube MCP and Composio, including event creation, event search, updates, deletion, attendee handling, invitations, and meeting-time discovery.
skill.summary: Manage Outlook Calendar workflows through Rube MCP tools.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance indicators, but the cited lines are Markdown tool names, calendar parameters, and examples rather than executable code. The real risk is the intended use of an external Rube MCP endpoint with Microsoft OAuth and calendar write actions that can create, modify, delete, or notify attendees.

medium_findings.title: External MCP and OAuth Calendar Access
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add an external Rube MCP endpoint and connect Outlook through Microsoft OAuth. This is expected functionality, but it expands the trust boundary to a third-party MCP service with access to calendar operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and OAuth connection steps are explicit in the setup section. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but it materially affects user data and account permissions.

medium_findings.title: Calendar Mutations Can Notify Attendees
medium_findings.description: The skill documents workflows that create, update, delete, decline, and reschedule events. Several notes warn that attendee changes and cancellations can send invitation or cancellation emails, so users should confirm destructive or outward-facing actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly describe invitation emails, attendee replacement, update notifications, deletion, and decline response behavior. These are intended functions, but mistakes can affect other users.

low_findings.title: Dismissed External Command Detections
low_findings.description: The static external command findings are false positives. The cited content uses Markdown backticks for Rube and Outlook tool names or example workflow text, and no shell, Ruby, or subprocess execution is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown instruction document and the flagged tokens are tool identifiers or calendar examples. No executable script block or command interpreter invocation was found.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Detections
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings are false positives. The flagged lines are calendar descriptions, timezone guidance, user identifiers, field names, and quick-reference entries, not cryptographic code or host inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited evidence is natural-language documentation for Outlook Calendar behavior. No hashing, encryption, system probing, or environment discovery logic appears in the reviewed file.

content.value_statement: Outlook scheduling often requires exact tool parameters, time zones, and attendee handling. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through reliable Rube MCP calendar workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of Outlook calendar events with subjects, times, locations, attendees, and Teams meeting options.
Explains how to list, search, and retrieve events using Outlook calendar views and OData filters.
Documents update, delete, decline, and reschedule workflows for existing calendar events.
Shows how to find meeting times and check free or busy schedules across attendees.
Calls out timezone handling, pagination, response nesting, and notification side effects.
limitations: Requires a connected Rube MCP server and an active Outlook OAuth connection.
Does not provide its own Microsoft Graph client or standalone automation code.
Tool schemas can change, so the skill instructs the assistant to search tools first.
Calendar write actions may send invitations, updates, or cancellations to attendees.

use_cases.title: Schedule Team Meetings
use_cases.description: Create Outlook events with the right attendees, time zone, Teams link, and meeting details.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Calendar Changes
use_cases.description: Find the correct event, update times or attendees, decline invitations, or delete obsolete meetings.

use_cases.title: Find Available Time Slots
use_cases.description: Use Outlook availability tools to compare attendee schedules and suggest workable meeting windows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Event
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Outlook calendar event for [subject] on [date] from [start time] to [end time] in [time zone]. Add [attendees] and [location].

prompt_templates.title: Find Events by Date
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my Outlook calendar events for [date range]. Use my mailbox time zone and include subject, start time, end time, and location.

prompt_templates.title: Update an Existing Meeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the Outlook event named [subject] during [date range]. Change it to [new time], preserve required attendees, and confirm before sending updates.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Group Availability
prompt_templates.prompt: Find meeting times for [attendee list] during [date range]. Search for [duration], respect work hours, and summarize the best options before creating an event.

output_examples.input: Create a Teams meeting with Alex and Priya tomorrow from 10:00 to 10:30 Eastern.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks current Outlook tool schemas, confirms the active Outlook connection, creates the event with Teams enabled, and reports the scheduled time and attendees.

output_examples.input: Move next Monday's project review to 2 PM and keep everyone invited.
output_examples.output: The assistant searches for matching events, verifies the exact event, warns that updates may notify attendees, and applies the new time after confirmation.

output_examples.input: Find a one-hour slot for the leadership team next week.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks attendee availability, compares suggested windows, and presents the strongest time options with any scheduling conflicts.

best_practices: Search Rube tools first so parameter names and schemas match the current MCP implementation.
Resolve the mailbox time zone before filtering or creating calendar events.
Confirm attendee lists, deletions, and notification behavior before making calendar changes.
anti_patterns: Do not assume event identifiers; search and verify the target event first.
Do not replace attendees without including every person who should remain invited.
Do not ignore time zone differences when using OData filters or calendar views.

faq.question: Does this skill require Rube MCP?
faq.answer: Yes. It is written for Rube MCP with the Composio Outlook toolkit.

faq.question: Can it create Microsoft Teams meetings?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents the Outlook parameters needed to request a Teams meeting link.

faq.question: Can it search existing calendar events?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains event listing, calendar views, filters, selected fields, and pagination.

faq.question: Can it delete events?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes deletion guidance and notes that cancellation notifications can be sent.

faq.question: Does it manage email messages?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflows focus on Outlook Calendar events and mailbox settings.

faq.question: Why should tools be searched first?
faq.answer: Rube tool schemas can change, so searching first helps the assistant use current parameters.
